mnemonics.title: SARA - Segmentation And Register Allocation
mnemonics.content: Remember: Segmentation Allocates variable-sized blocks (Segments), Paging uses fixed-size blocks (Pages). Think 'SARA' = Segments Are Reasonable, Always fixed pages.

mnemonics.title: VIP Tables Have Large Entries
mnemonics.content: Virtual Index Problems: VPN (Virtual Page Number) selects entry from Page Table. TLAs (Three Letter Acronyms): VA has VPN + offset, PA has PFN (Page Frame Number).

mnemonics.title: TLB Miss Means Page Table Search
mnemonics.content: TLB: Translation Lookaside Buffer - fast cache. On TLB miss, walk page table. On page fault, load from disk. Order: TLB → Page Table → MMU → Physical Memory.

acronyms.usage: Hardware component that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses using page/segment tables.
Cache of recent virtual-to-physical address translations; speeds up memory access by avoiding page table walks.
Processor register pointing to the base of the current process's page table in memory.
Address used by programs; consists of segment number + offset (segmentation) or page number + offset (paging).
Actual address in RAM hardware; result of address translation by MMU.
The page number portion of a virtual address; used as index into page table.
The frame number portion of a physical address; identifies the physical page in RAM.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a rectangle divided into two parts: LEFT = VPN (page number), RIGHT = offset (within page). The offset bits determine page size (e.g., 12 bits = 4KB pages). Arrow shows VPN maps to PFN via page table.
Draw a three-stage conversion: Virtual Address = Segment# + VPN + Offset → Segment Table (gives base of page table) → Page Table (maps VPN to PFN) → Physical Address = PFN + Offset.
Draw a box representing one PTE showing: Valid bit, Modified (dirty) bit, Reference bit, Protection bits, Frame number. Add a 'present/absent' bit - if 0, page is on disk (causes page fault).

analogies.visualization: Think of RAM as a library with fixed-size book slots (frames). Each book (process page) is copied into any available slot. The index card system (page table) tells you which slot holds which book.

An encyclopedia has variable-sized volumes (segments): History might be 5 inches thick, Science only 2 inches. Each volume starts at a different page number - that's the segment base. You need both volume number (segment) and page within it (offset) to find info.

Instead of looking up every contact in a phone directory (page table in memory), you keep frequently used numbers in your personal book (TLB). It's faster but limited in size - sometimes you still need the directory.

When you need a book not in the library (page not in RAM), you fill out a request form (page fault). The librarian (OS) orders it from storage (disk), potentially evicting an old book if library is full.
